coredata.dc:title: Thermodynamic re-evaluation of the C–Ni–Ti system
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Isomäki, I.
Hämäläinen, M.
coredata.dc:description: Thermodynamic re-evaluation of the C–Ni–Ti system was carried out in order to make replacing of expensive cobalt possible in cemented carbides. No ternary compound exists in C–Ni–Ti system. Re-evaluation was made in order to maintain consistency in C–Ni–Ti–W system. Lattice sites were calculated in integers, except NiTi (B2_bcc) and Dis_B2 (disordered Ti), which were made with decimal numbers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C–Ni–Ti system
Thermodynamic
Re-evaluation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluated thermodynamic parameters for the C–Ni–Ti system [J/mol]

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Calculated C–Ni–Ti phase diagram at 1173K with experimental data points.
Calculated C–Ni–Ti phase diagram at 1873K with experimental data points.

article.ce:title: Thermodynamic re-evaluation of the C–Ni–Ti system

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Helsinki University of Technology, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, P.O. Box 6200, 02015 HUT, Finland

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thermodynamic re-evaluation of the C–Ni–Ti system was carried out in order to make replacing of expensive cobalt possible in cemented carbides. No ternary compound exists in C–Ni–Ti system. Re-evaluation was made in order to maintain consistency in C–Ni–Ti–W system. Lattice sites were calculated in integers, except NiTi (B2_bcc) and Dis_B2 (disordered Ti), which were made with decimal numbers.

ce:section.ce:para: The C–Ni–Ti system was re-evaluated in this paper. The system in question was a part of the C–Ni–Ti–W alloy and the thermodynamic parameters had to be changed consistent for other ternary systems in C–Ni–Ti–W, which can be used as cemented carbides for cutting and drilling tools. Final aim of the work will be to get cheaper binder phases for hard metals when nickel can be used as a substitute for cobalt.

The Gibbs energies of pure elements with respect to temperature °G i(T)= G i(T)−
H i
SER
, are represented by Eq. (1):
(1)
°
G i
( T ) = a + b T + c T
ln ( T ) + d
T 2
+ e
T
− 1
+ f
T 3
+ i
T 4
+ j
T 7
+ k
T
− 9
The °G i data are referred to the constant enthalpy value of the so-called Standard Element Reference
H i
SER
at 298.15K and 1bar as recommended by Scientific Group Thermodata Europe (SGTE), Dinsdale [1]. These reference states are graphite (C), fcc_A1 (Ni) and hcp_A3 (Ti). The °G i expressions may be given for several temperature ranges, where the coefficients a, b, c, d, e, f, i, j and k have different values. The °G i(T) functions of the pure elements C, Ni and Ti were taken from Dinsdale [1].

The solution phases, i.e. liquid, B2_bcc, Dis_B2, bcc_A2, graphite, fcc_A1 and hcp_A3, were described by adopting a substitutional solution model, and the Gibbs energy of the phase Φ was expressed as follows:
(2)
G m
Φ
=
x C
°
G C
Φ
+
x
Ni

Φ

Ni
Φ
+ R T (
x C
ln ⁡
x C
+
x
Ni
ln ⁡
x
Ni
+
x

ln ⁡
x
Ti
) +
G m
Φ
E
where x i is the mole fraction of the element i and
°
G C
Φ
,
°
G
Ni
Φ
and
°
G
Ti
Φ
are the molar Gibbs energies of C, Ni and Ti, respectively, with the crystal structure of Φ, where °G i is defined by Eq. (1). The excess Gibbs energy,
G m
Φ
E
, was treated as follows:
(3)
G m
Φ
E
=
x C
x
Ni
L
C : Ni
Φ
+

Ti

C : Ti
Φ
+
x
Ni
x
Ti
L
Ni : Ti
Φ
+
x C
x
Ni
x
Ti
L
C : Ni : Ti
Φ
(4)
L
n ,
Φ
=
a
n ,
Φ
+
b
n ,
Φ
T
where a
n,Φ and b
n,Φ are the interaction parameters, which were taken to be temperature dependent.

The phase B2_bcc is a ordered phase and Dis_B2 is a combined disordered phase. Compounds TiC, Ni3Ti and NiTi2 were modelled with two sublattice model. TiC has one lattice with Ni,Ti atoms and second with C,Va, Ni3Ti has Ni,Ti in both lattices and NiTi2 has Ni in first lattice and Ti,C in second. The Ni–Ti system was taken from Sundman's work [2], except parameters for Ni3Ti and NiTi2 phases, which were taken from Zeng et al. [3]. bcc_A2 phase was a ternary C–Ni–Ti bcc phase. B2_bcc phase had to be made in Thermo-calc. In Thermo-calc GES module, one had to use amend-phase-description command for this phase. Ti–C system was made by Balasubramanian [4]. C–Ni system was made by Lee [5] and bcc_A2 was modified by Ansara [6]. Models and species are shown in Table 1 .

The evaluation was carried out using the Redlich–Kister polynomial and the sublattice model for the phase descriptions. The sublimation point of carbon was evaluated as 4099K by Massalski [7], the melting point for nickel was 1728K [7] and for titanium 1943K [7]. Du and Schuster [8] made a review of the experiments done for this system. They heated certain alloys at 1173K and analysed them using XRD and SEM/EDX techniques. von Schenk [9] studied liquid+graphite phase area in the C–Ni–Ti system at 1873K [9]. Eremenko [10] studied the Ni–TiC quasibinary system using DTA. They observed liquidus, solidus and solvus lines at nickel rich end. The greatest difference between Eremenko et al. [12] and Stover and Wulff [11] was that Eremenko et al. [12] observed Ni–TiC equilibrium, but Stover and Wulff [11] observed fcc_A1-(Ni)+C intermediate phase region at temperatures below 1553K (Fig. 3).

The most recent literature was scanned from the THERMET literature database created by Ansara [13] and Landolt [14]. The calculations were carried out using the Thermo-Calc software (version P). Fig. 1 shows the ternary C–Ni–Ti phase diagram at 1173K.

In Fig. 1, the fit of experimental data points was reasonable. Data were taken from Du and Schuster [8]. Fig. 2 shows the C–Ni–Ti phase diagram at 1873K with experimental data points. The experimental data points were taken from von Schenk [9].
In Fig. 2, the fit of data points was reasonable, but only few points were available. Fig. 3 shows quasibinary Ni–TiC equilibrium.

In Fig. 3, data were collected from three different sources [10–12] and fit was reasonable. The greatest disagreement with references was equilibrium of fcc-Ni at low temperatures. Eremenko et al. [12] observed the fcc-Ni phase with equilibrium of TiC. Stover and Wulff [11] found C+fcc_A1-(Ni) intermediate phase between fcc_A1-(Ni) and TiC phases. First three point sets were taken from Eremenko [10], next five sets were taken from Stover and Wulff [11] and last set were taken from Eremenko et al. [12]. C+fcc-(Ni) area is from Stover and Wulff [11] and is marked on the Fig. 3.

Due to lack of experimental data and simplified models made evaluation difficult. The binary Ni–C and Ti–C systems were quite easy to do, but the Ni–Ti system had combined ordered and disordered bcc phases. The C–Ni–Ti system had no ternary compounds. There was no indication that the solubility of nickel in TiC was above a fraction of 1at.% [11]. NiTi2 compound dissolved carbon about 1.5at.% C at 1143K by Stover and Wulff [11] and Du and Schuster [8] estimated that the solubility of carbon in NiTi2 was about 4.0at.% at 1173K. Experimental error in Du and Schuster [8] was estimated as 1–2at.%. Calculated amount of dissolving carbon in NiTi2 at 1173K was about 3.0at.% C. Du and Schuster [8] also assessed thermodynamically C–Ni–Ti system. Ni–C was taken from Lee [5], Ti–C from Dumitrescu et al. [15] and Ni–Ti from Bellen et al. [16]. Dumitrescu et al. [15] made an internal report of KTH in Sweden about Ti–C system. Bellen et al. [16] assessed all Ni–Ti compounds with decimal sublattice fractions. In the present work, integer sublattice fractions have been used, except for phase B2_bcc, which was an ordered phase and Dis_B2, which was a combined disordered phase. Sundman [2] assessed these phases using decimal sublattice fractions. Integers have been used for consistency in C–Ni–Ti–W system, but B2_bcc and Dis_B2 have been made as in Sundman's work for simplicity. Du and Schuster [8] had ternary parameters for liquid, fcc_A1 and NiTi2 phases. The authors used ternary parameters for liquid, fcc_A1, TiC and NiTi2 phases. TiC was separated from fcc_A1 for simplicity. Du and Schuster [8], had used three sublattice model for NiTi2 as (Ni)1(Ti)2(C,Va)0.5. Du and Schuster [8] had fictitious ternary compound Ni1Ti2C0.5, but in this work this compound was neglected and two sublattice solution phase model as (Ni)(Ti,C)2 was used.

The C–Ni–Ti system was re-evaluated in this paper. The system in question was a part of the C–Ni–Ti–W alloy and the thermodynamic parameters had to be changed consistent for other ternary systems in C–Ni–Ti–W, which can be used as cemented carbides for cutting and drilling tools.

The thermodynamic system under consideration was C–Ni–Ti–W, in which the ternary C–Ni–Ti was only a ternary subsystem. Ni–Ti system was taken from Sundman [2] combined with Zeng et al. work [3]. Ti–C was created from Balasubramanian [4] and C–Ni was by Lee [5] modified by Ansara [6].

The evaluation of the ternary C–Ni–Ti system was quite reasonable. A total of 10 different phases were evaluated and the simplest possible interaction parameters were used. The optimization of the ordered–disordered bcc_A2-Dis_B2 was considered to be so difficult that it was taken directly from Sundman's work [2]. Quasibinary Ni–TiC system had a problem at low temperatures. It was still unclear if there a C-fcc_A1-(Ni) intermediate phase between fcc_A1-Ni and TiC or not in this system.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thanks to Laboratory of Materials Manufacturing and Powder Metallurgy in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering at Helsinki University of Technology.
